children.name: Chamaeleo
children.description: The true chameleons of the Old World — masters of active colour change through chromatophores, iridophores, and melanophores arranged in layers within the skin. Contrary to popular belief, chameleons do not change colour primarily for camouflage; colour change communicates social status, temperature, and emotion — a submissive male rapidly fades to pale, while a dominant male blazes with vivid signals. Eyes move independently, providing simultaneous nearly-360° vision. The projectile tongue, up to twice the body length, accelerates at 264 g to strike prey.

children.name: Chamaeleo chamaeleon
children.description: The only chameleon native to Europe, found in Iberia, southern Greece, and the Mediterranean islands. Oviparous; buries eggs in soil. The northernmost-occurring chameleon species.

children.name: Chamaeleo calyptratus
children.description: Native to Yemen and Saudi Arabia. The dramatic casque (helmet) on the head channels morning dew and rainwater into the mouth — an adaptation for an arid environment. The most common chameleon in captivity.

children.name: Furcifer
children.description: Malagasy chameleons — a large and spectacular genus endemic almost entirely to Madagascar, a continent-island that is a global hotspot of chameleon diversity, hosting nearly half of all chameleon species. The Panther Chameleon shows extraordinary geographic colour variation: males from different regions ('locales') differ so dramatically in colour that they were long thought to be different species. Females signal receptivity and pregnancy state through distinctive colour changes.

children.name: Furcifer pardalis
children.description: Males show extreme geographic colour variation across Madagascar — red-and-blue from Ambilobe, turquoise-and-green from Nosy Be, orange from Ambanja. A 2016 study confirmed that what were thought to be one species may actually be 11 distinct lineages.

children.name: Furcifer lateralis
children.description: One of the most beautiful chameleons — females show a striking pattern of white spots and turquoise on green, orange, and black depending on state. Found in open grassland and agricultural areas across much of Madagascar.

children.name: Furcifer oustaleti
children.description: One of the largest chameleons in the world, reaching 68 cm. Found across a wide range of habitats in Madagascar. Males are relatively drab — grey-brown — compared to most Furcifer species.

children.name: Trioceros
children.description: Three-horned chameleons — an African genus famous for the three prominent rostral horns of the males, used in combat. Jackson's Chameleon is native to East Africa but has established a large feral population in Hawaii following the accidental release of captive animals in the 1970s. Meller's Chameleon is the largest chameleon in mainland Africa. Most Trioceros species are viviparous — giving birth to live young rather than laying eggs, an unusual trait among chameleons.

children.name: Trioceros jacksonii
children.description: Named for Sir Frederick John Jackson, colonial administrator and ornithologist. Males bear three horns — one rostral and two preocular — used in shoving contests for territory. The feral Hawaiian population is considered an invasive species.

children.name: Trioceros melleri
children.description: The largest chameleon on mainland Africa, reaching up to 60 cm. A solitary inhabitant of highland East African forest. The small, single rostral horn distinguishes it from the three-horned species.

children.name: Brookesia
children.description: Leaf chameleons — tiny, leaf-litter-dwelling chameleons of Madagascar, some of which are the smallest reptiles and tetrapods on Earth. Brookesia nana, described in 2021, is the world's smallest reptile: adult males measure just 13.5 mm from snout to vent. These miniaturised chameleons are thought to be cryptically patterned to mimic dead leaves, and lack the dramatic colour-change ability of larger species. Their tiny size and specialised habitat make them extremely vulnerable to forest clearance.

children.name: Brookesia micra
children.description: Described in 2012 from the islet of Nosy Hara, Madagascar. At the time of description, the smallest chameleon and one of the smallest reptiles in the world. Adults sleep on low vegetation at night, returning to the leaf litter by day.

children.name: Brookesia nana
children.description: Described in 2021 from the Sorata massif of northern Madagascar. The male holotype is 13.5 mm snout–vent — the smallest known adult of any reptile species. The female is almost twice as long, an extreme case of sexual size dimorphism.

children.name: Calumma
children.description: Calumma chameleons — a genus of largely arboreal, forest-dwelling chameleons endemic to Madagascar, characterised by elaborate head ornamentation including rostral appendages, casques, and crests. Parson's Chameleon is the largest chameleon in the world by weight and one of the longest-lived chameleons, reaching 10+ years. It is slow-maturing and slow-reproducing, making it particularly sensitive to collection for the pet trade.

children.name: Calumma parsonii
children.description: The heaviest chameleon and one of the longest-lived lizards. Males can reach 68 cm and 700 g. Restricted to humid primary rainforest in eastern Madagascar; highly sensitive to habitat disturbance. Export is now banned.
